original_abstract: Direct electrochemical propylene epoxidation by means of water-oxidation intermediates presents a sustainable alternative to existing routes that involve hazardous chlorine or peroxide reagents. We report an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtO x /C), which reaches a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% toward propylene epoxidation at 50 milliamperes per square centimeter at ambient temperature and pressure. Embedding platinum into the palladium oxide crystal structure stabilized oxidized platinum species, resulting in improved catalyst performance. The reaction kinetics suggest that epoxidation on PdPtO x /C proceeds through electrophilic attack by metal-bound peroxo intermediates. This work demonstrates an effective strategy for selective electrochemical oxygen-atom transfer from water, without mediators, for diverse oxygenation reactions.
humanized_abstract: Direct electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-oxidation intermediates has emerged as a potentially safer and more sustainable approach compared to chlorine or peroxide based processes. Here we show that an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst PdPtO x /C with an average Pd/Pt ratio of 2 yields a faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% toward selective propylene oxidation at a current density of 50 mA cm −2 at ambient pressure and temperature. Enhanced catalytic performance was realized by the incorporation of platinum into the palladium oxide structure, resulting in the formation of stabilized oxidized platinum species. Catalyst kinetics analysis indicates that propylene oxidation on PdPtO x /C proceeds via a metal-centered electrophilic mechanism that involves the interaction of propylene with metal-bound peroxo species derived from water oxidation. Our study presents a catalyst system that achieves selective electrochemical oxygen-atom transfer to substrates from water media without the need of redox-active catalyst mediators.

Catalyst kinetics analysis indicates that propylene oxidation on PdPtO x /C proceeds via a metal-centered electrophilic mechanism that involves the interaction of propylene with metal-bound peroxo species derived from water oxidation. Our study presents a catalyst system that achieves selective electrochemical oxygen-atom transfer to substrates from water media without the need of redox-active catalyst mediators.
